title: Agent mirror-sync gate is inert — points at deleted checker path, silently warns-and-skips
evidence: L40 MIRROR_CHECKER="$REPO_ROOT/.opencode/skills/deep-improvement/scripts/check-agent-mirror-sync.cjs" — `ls` confirms MISSING; real file now at .opencode/skills/deep-loop-workflows/deep-improvement/scripts/check-agent-mirror-sync.cjs. L48-49 `[[ ! -f "$MIRROR_CHECKER" ]]` → echoes WARNING and falls through (no exit) to exit 0.
why: When staged agent files are committed the local gate no longer runs check-agent-mirror-sync.cjs, so a desynced .opencode/.claude/.codex mirror can be committed with only a stderr warning — a protective gate is dead, not failing closed.

title: Dead drift-guard path + broken runnable npx command (deleted deep-improvement/scripts)
evidence: L300 names drift guard `.opencode/skills/deep-improvement/scripts/skill-benchmark/tests/sk-code-router-sync.vitest.ts` and tells reader to run `npx vitest run skill-benchmark/tests/sk-code-router-sync.vitest.ts` from `.opencode/skills/deep-improvement/scripts` — that cwd no longer exists, so the cd/npx fails.
why: Doc points to a non-existent test path and a repro command whose working dir is gone; the named drift guard can't be located or run from the cited location.
fix: Repoint to .opencode/skills/deep-loop-workflows/deep-improvement/scripts/skill-benchmark/tests/sk-code-router-sync.vitest.ts and update the `from` cwd to .opencode/skills/deep-loop-workflows/deep-improvement/scripts.

title: Two runnable `node` commands invoke deleted loop-host.cjs path
evidence: L52 and L62 both run `node .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/scripts/shared/loop-host.cjs ...` (router mode L52, live mode L62); that script path is under the deleted deep-improvement/scripts tree → node fails with 'Cannot find module'.
why: Copy-paste QUICK START repro for the sk-code skill benchmark is broken; both modes error out on a missing module.
fix: Replace path prefix with .opencode/skills/deep-loop-workflows/deep-improvement/scripts/shared/loop-host.cjs in both commands.

title: Reproduce block `cd`s into deleted deep-improvement/scripts dir
evidence: L29 `cd .opencode/skills/deep-improvement/scripts` inside the Reproduce fenced block; dir is deleted so cd fails and the subsequent `node skill-benchmark/run-skill-benchmark.cjs ...` never runs.
why: d4r-live reproduction instructions are unusable as written.

title: Test reads deleted deep-ai-council changelog from disk — now a missing-file path
evidence: L27 changelog_path = REPO_ROOT / ".opencode/skills/deep-ai-council/changelog/v1.0.0.0.md"; then L29 validate_document(str(changelog_path)) and L31-32 assert document_type=='changelog' and blocking_errors==[]. `ls` confirms old file MISSING; it moved to .opencode/skills/deep-loop-workflows/ai-council/changelog/v1.0.0.0.md. Unlike the synthetic-string case, this path is dereferenced on disk.
why: test_real_skill_changelog_has_no_blocking_errors now validates a non-existent file, so the pytest case fails/errors instead of exercising the real changelog — a live test breakage from the merge.

title: Council-matrix test validates deleted deep-ai-council dir
evidence: L14 `python3 .opencode/skills/sk-doc/scripts/quick_validate.py .opencode/skills/deep-ai-council` — `ls -d` confirms .opencode/skills/deep-ai-council MISSING (moved to deep-loop-workflows/ai-council); quick_validate.py is handed a non-existent target dir.
why: Runnable test helper points at a deleted directory, so the council validation step no longer exercises the real (relocated) ai-council skill.

title: Stale deep-review/SKILL.md sample path in scope-policy test (inert — test still passes)
evidence: L515 expect(shouldIndexForCodeGraph('/root/.opencode/skills/deep-review/SKILL.md', config.scopePolicy)).toBe(false); the '/root/...' string is a synthetic input to scope-policy logic — NOT read from disk — and deep-review isn't in includedSkillsList, so it correctly returns false regardless. Verified via Read, not assumed.
why: Reference is stale (deep-review skill was deleted/merged) but functionally harmless: the test does not touch the filesystem and still passes; only the example name is now misleading to readers.
fix: Optional: swap the sample path to a still-existing excluded skill (e.g. deep-loop-workflows) for clarity; no functional change required.

title: Destructive 5-skill deletion shipped while its own release-blocking P0 gates (B1 council-graph coverage + the deletion gate) were never run — parent falsely claims Complete
evidence: Deletion SHIPPED: all 5 dirs (deep-ai-council, deep-context, deep-improvement, deep-research, deep-review) gone from .opencode/skills/ and absent from `git status` (committed). B1 NEVER implemented: doctor_deep-loop.yaml references deep-loop-graph.sqlite 4x (L79,80,145,163) and council-graph 0x ('ZERO council references'), yet council-graph.sqlite is a real 81920-byte on-disk DB (database/council-graph.sqlite) with `DB_FILENAME = 'council-graph.sqlite'` at lib/council/council-graph-db.ts:81 and a full lib/council/ impl. 009 checklist CHK-023 [P0] '/doctor deep-loop covers both deep-loop-graph.sqlite and council-graph.sqlite' = unchecked [ ]; CHK-026 [P0] 'Exactly five old skill directories are deleted' = unchecked [ ]; Verification Summary L148 = P0 0/18, P1 0/6, P2 0/2, 'pending execution'. 009 spec.md:54 mandates B1 'must be resolved before the council mode ships unmonitored' and deletion happens 'only here, after every prior gate is green'. R4 contradiction: all 9 children spec.md:41 = 'Status | Planned (scaffold)', completion_pct:0, sha256:0000... fingerprints; 004-009 have NO implementation-summary.md (only 001/002/003 do); parent spec.md:41 = 'Status | Complete' with phase table L90-98 marking all 9 phases Complete.
why: An irreversible, committed deletion of 5 production skill surfaces was performed without running ANY of its 18 release-blocking P0 gates — including the one gate (CHK-023/B1) whose explicit purpose was to ensure council mode is not left unmonitored after the old skills are gone; council mode now ships with a real coverage DB that /doctor cannot see, exactly the unmonitored-ship the spec forbade. The parent 'Complete' claim masks that every child is an unexecuted 0% scaffold, so the gate failure is invisible to resume/validation and to any operator trusting the parent status.
fix: Treat 152 as NOT shipped: revert parent spec.md status to in-progress (children are scaffolds, completion_pct 0), implement B1 by adding council-graph.sqlite probes to doctor_deep-loop.yaml mirroring the deep-loop-graph.sqlite checks (L79,80,145,163), then actually execute 009's gate pipeline and record evidence for all 18 P0 items (esp. CHK-023, CHK-026) before re-confirming the deletion; if deletion cannot be ungated, restore the 5 dirs from git history until the gate set passes.

title: Adopted lock provides near-zero concurrent-session exclusion: owner PID is dead-on-arrival with no refresh, so the on_acquire 'fail closed' guard effectively never fires
evidence: acquire cmd (deep_review_auto.yaml:238, mirrored in research/ai-council/context YAMLs) passes NO --owner-pid; resolveOwnerPid then defaults to process.pid (loop-lock.cjs:96-98), and the runtime CLI re-execs under a tsx child (loop-lock.cjs:43-56) so owner_pid = the ephemeral child PID that exits the instant the command returns. No YAML has a refresh step (only acquire+release confirmed via rg). isStaleLoopLock therefore returns true via '!processAlive(data.ownerPid)' (loop-lock.ts:224) immediately, so a genuinely concurrent same-packet session hits the reclaim path and gets {acquired:true} (loop-lock.ts:248-249); the on_acquire 'acquired=false -> fail closed - another deep-review run already holds this packet' (deep_review_auto.yaml:239) only fires inside the sub-second window the first acquire process is still alive.
why: The lock is the only structural guard against two concurrent loops corrupting shared packet state (state.jsonl, findings-registry); the per-step 'fail closed' wording implies mutual exclusion that the dead-owner wiring does not deliver. Partially disclosed by the advisory note at line 240 ('advisory - best-effort single-writer, not a hard mutex'), so this is over-claim/defense-in-depth-only, not a silent break.
fix: Either pass a stable long-lived --owner-pid (the host loop's own PID) at acquire and add a periodic refresh step, or soften the on_acquire 'fail closed - another run holds this packet' wording to match the advisory reality already stated in the note.

title: Reclaim is single-winner among reclaimers (correct), but a latent TOCTOU exists between a reclaimer and a concurrently-refreshing live owner
evidence: Reclaim logic: 'renameSync(lockPath, reclaimPath)' then on ENOENT 'return false' (165-171) then 'writeLoopLockExclusive(lockPath, data)' under O_EXCL (175); two reclaimers cannot both win because only one renames the inode and the vacated path is O_EXCL-created — the seat's 'can two reclaimers both win?' = NO. However acquireLoopLock's staleness read (235-236) and the reclaim rename (165) are not atomic against refreshLoopLock -> writeLoopLockAtomic (269 -> 111-125, temp+rename) issued by a live-but-late owner: the reclaimer can rename away the owner's just-refreshed fresh lock and then O_EXCL-create its own, yielding two simultaneous holders.
why: Genuine cross-process race in the library, but UNREACHABLE in current adoption because no YAML mode issues a refresh (owner never refreshes), so the colliding write never happens in deployed config. Worth fixing only if a long-lived-owner + refresh model is adopted per W2-C-1.
fix: If refresh is introduced, re-verify staleness after the reclaim rename (re-read reclaimed file and confirm it is still the stale holder) before taking the vacated path, or gate refresh and reclaim on the same O_EXCL token.

title: Cross-process test only covers the fresh O_EXCL path; the atomic-rename stale-reclaim single-winner path is never exercised concurrently — and the known flake is NOT masked
evidence: The only multi-process test spawns two children that both acquire FRESH locks (144-166), exercising writeLoopLockExclusive/O_EXCL, with strict assertions: exactly one acquired, one not (158-159), disk packet_id == winner and all children agree (161-162). The stale-reclaim rename single-winner path is tested ONLY single-process/sequentially ('reclaims a lock whose owner pid is dead', 192-203). The documented 'known loop-lock timing flake' is this cross-process test failing spuriously under spawn latency (barrier deadline 'Date.now()+2000' at line 70 vs --experimental-strip-types child startup); assertions stay strict, there is no .skip/.todo/retry (rg clean) and the playbook documents it openly (fanout-single-executor-parity-research.md:57,63) — so the flake is genuinely NOT masked, it is a false-FAILURE not a hidden false-PASS.
why: The most race-prone path (concurrent reclaimers of a stale lock, i.e. the exact 'two reclaimers both win' scenario the seat asked about) has no cross-process regression test, so a future regression in tryReclaimStaleLoopLock would pass CI. The flake itself is harmless and honestly disclosed.
fix: Add a cross-process test mirroring lines 144-166 but seeding a stale (dead-owner) lock so both children take the reclaim path, asserting exactly one reclaim winner; optionally raise the barrier deadline / poll-spawn to de-flake the existing fresh test.
